title: Speech and language therapists
description: Assess and treat persons with speech, language, voice, and fluency disorders. May select alternative communication systems and teach their use. May perform research related to speech and language problems. Assess and treat persons with hearing and related disorders. May fit hearing aids and provide auditory training. May perform research related to hearing problems.

tasks.task: Monitor patients' progress and adjust treatments accordingly, and provide ongoing observation of hearing or balance status.
Supervise or collaborate with therapy team and supervise students or assistants as needed.

Counsel and instruct patients and their families in techniques to improve hearing and communication related to hearing loss, and educate patients and family members about communication techniques to cope with or avoid misunderstandings.

Evaluate hearing and balance disorders to determine diagnoses and courses of treatment, and evaluate hearing or speech and language test results, medical or background information to diagnose and plan treatment for speech, language, fluency, voice, or swallowing disorders.

Program and monitor cochlear implants to fit the needs of patients, fit, dispense, and repair assistive devices such as hearing aids, and recommend assistive devices according to patients' needs or nature of impairments.

Conduct or direct research on hearing or speech topics and report findings to help in the development of procedures, technologies, or treatments, and participate in conferences, training, or publish research results to share knowledge of new treatment methods.
Plan and conduct treatment programs for patients' hearing or balance problems, consulting with multidisciplinary teams, educators, physicians, nurses, and other healthcare personnel as necessary.

Administer hearing tests and examine patients to collect information on type and degree of impairment using specialized instruments and electronic equipment, and administer hearing or speech and language evaluations to gather data on impairments.

Instruct clients in techniques for more effective communication, such as sign language, lip reading, or voice improvement, and teach clients to control or strengthen tongue, jaw, face muscles, or breathing mechanisms.

Consult with and advise educators or medical staff on hearing or speech topics, and develop individual or group activities or programs in schools to address behavior, speech, language, or swallowing problems.
Engage in marketing activities to promote business for private practices.
Maintain patient records at all stages, including initial and subsequent evaluation and treatment activities, write reports and maintain proper documentation of patient information.
Assure and develop hearing screening programs, and educate and supervise audiology students and healthcare personnel.
Measure noise levels in workplaces and conduct hearing conservation programs in industry, military, schools, and communities.
Refer patients to additional medical or educational services, if needed, and consult with and refer clients to additional medical or educational services.
Perform administrative tasks, such as managing office functions and finances, and complete administrative responsibilities such as coordinating paperwork or scheduling activities.
Conduct lessons or direct educational or therapeutic games to assist teachers dealing with speech problems, and communicate with students who use an alternative method of communication.
Provide communication instruction to dialect speakers or students with limited English proficiency.
Use computer applications to identify or assist with communication disabilities.
